steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure requiring connection
steps.content:
The sentence has two independent clauses that need joining:
“It began to dim over the next few weeks” (main clause completed) and “supernovas are short-lived phenomena” (main clause following the dependent clause “although they are extremely powerful”). The task is choosing how to connect them.

steps.title: Apply the rule for conjunctive adverbs
steps.content: When joining two independent clauses with a conjunctive adverb like “however,” the standard pattern is: semicolon before the adverb, comma after. This produces: It began to dim over the next few weeks; however, although they are extremely powerful, supernovas are short-lived phenomena. Option C uses only a comma before “however,” which violates this rule. Option D places a comma before and semicolon after, which reverses the correct pattern. Option B creates two separate sentences—acceptable but not the SAT's preferred pattern when a close relationship between clauses exists. A is correct.
